settings.title: Malin Dhamsara
settings.tagline: Web developer, Electronics enthusiast, ML enthusiast

data.title: Personal Portfolio
data.summary: This site — a minimal portfolio and blog built on a TypeScript CMS, designed in Figma and rendered with Astro.

$media.alt: Portfolio website on a laptop screen

children.text: I wanted a portfolio that I own end to end — content, design, and infrastructure — without giving up the convenience of a CMS. The result is this site: an Astro front end backed by EmDash, with every page server-rendered.
The stack
Astro for rendering, Tailwind CSS for styling, SQLite for storage, and an admin panel for editing. The design started as four Figma frames and was translated into a small, token-driven design system.

data.title: Line Follower Robot
data.summary: An autonomous line-following robot built around a custom PCB and a PID control loop tuned on the track.

$media.alt: Close-up of a small robot circuit board

children.text: The brief was simple: follow a black line around a track as fast as possible without leaving it. The interesting part was everything underneath — sensor calibration, motor control, and a PID loop that had to be tuned by hand.
Hardware
An array of IR reflectance sensors feeds into a microcontroller that drives two DC motors through an H-bridge. I designed the board, soldered it, and wrote the firmware in C.

data.title: Digit Classifier
data.summary: A handwritten digit recognizer trained from scratch — first with NumPy, then with a proper framework — to understand what the abstractions hide.

children.text: Everyone's first neural network recognizes digits. I did it twice: once with nothing but NumPy, deriving backpropagation by hand, and once with a modern framework to appreciate what it does for you.
What I learned
Writing gradient descent from scratch teaches you more in a weekend than a month of copying tutorials. The NumPy version reached 97% accuracy — slow, but mine.

data.title: Task CLI
data.summary: A tiny command-line task manager that stores everything in a single plain-text file — grep-able, git-able, yours.

children.text: I kept losing tasks in apps that wanted accounts, sync, and subscriptions. So I wrote the opposite: a single binary, a single text file, and commands that do exactly one thing.
Design
The file format is human-readable by design, so version control gives you history for free and any text editor is a valid client. The CLI is just a fast interface over it.

data.title: Own your tools
data.excerpt: Why I rebuilt my portfolio on a CMS I control instead of another hosted page builder.

children.text: Every few years I rebuild my personal site. Each time the same question comes up: rent or own? Hosted builders are fast, but the content lives in someone else's database, behind someone else's pricing page.
This time the answer was a small TypeScript CMS running on SQLite. The whole site — content included — fits in a git repository and a single database file. If I ever move hosts, I copy two files.
The lesson generalizes: for things you expect to keep for a decade, prefer boring formats and tools you can inspect. Convenience is rented; plain text is owned.

data.title: Learning in public
data.excerpt: Notes on writing down what I learn, and why the audience is mostly future me.

children.text: The best study habit I have picked up is writing short notes about things as I learn them — not polished tutorials, just honest records of what confused me and what unstuck me.
Six months later, those notes are the fastest way back into a topic. Future me is the most frequent reader of this blog, and he is grateful for specifics: commands, error messages, dead ends.

data.title: What soldering taught me about software
data.excerpt: Hardware has no undo. Thinking before committing turns out to be a transferable skill.

children.text: When you solder a component backwards, there is no ctrl-z — there is a desoldering pump and ten minutes of regret. Hardware punishes haste in a way software rarely does.

I noticed my code changing after a semester of electronics labs: I read datasheets — sorry, documentation — before writing anything, and I test assumptions at the boundary before building on top of them.
Measure twice, cut once. It turns out the old advice works on keyboards too.
